title: Energy Bill Hikes 'Inescapable' if Oil Stays High, Warns British Gas Boss
description: Centrica CEO Chris O'Shea warns that household energy bills will inevitably rise if oil prices remain elevated due to conflict in the Middle East.
lede: Household energy bills will inevitably face an "inescapable" increase if oil prices remain high amid the ongoing Iran war, according to the boss of British Gas-owner Centrica. Crude prices have already surged 45% to $106 a barrel since the conflict began, threatening to hit consumers hard by July.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Chris O'Shea, CEO of Centrica, told the BBC that the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has severely disrupted oil supplies, making future bill hikes almost certain if the situation persists. While gas supplies have been less affected, the overall energy market is feeling the squeeze from the geopolitical instability.

Consultancy Cornwall Insight has forecast that energy bills in England, Scotland, and Wales could rise by an average of £332 starting in July. This sharp increase would wipe out the brief relief consumers saw from the £117 drop in the energy price cap earlier in April.

Since the onset of the US-Israel war with Iran, global crude oil prices have rocketed by 45%, reaching $106 a barrel. The Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint through which roughly 20% of the world's oil typically flows, has seen shipping effectively brought to a standstill.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The stark warning from the UK's largest energy supplier highlights the direct vulnerability of domestic households to overseas conflicts. Even though only 3-4% of the global gas supply has been lost compared to the massive hit on oil, the interconnected nature of energy markets means consumers will still pay the price.

O'Shea noted that the immediate impact will likely be felt more acutely at the petrol pumps than in household gas bills. However, the broader inflationary pressure threatens to exacerbate the ongoing cost-of-living crisis for millions of families across the UK.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The crisis has prompted urgent high-level discussions, with the prime minister convening an emergency meeting involving senior ministers and the Bank of England governor to strategize on countering the economic fallout. The government has already deployed a £53 million package targeted at homes struggling with soaring heating oil costs.

Debate is currently raging over how best to manage the profits of energy giants during this volatile period. While the government's cost-of-living tsar suggested temporarily capping energy company profits, Housing Secretary Steve Reed stated such an intervention is not currently deemed necessary. O'Shea himself pushed back against further caps, pointing to the existing 78% windfall tax on North Sea oil and gas extractors.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Centrica is advocating for increased oil and gas exploration in the North Sea, alongside expanded gas and battery storage, to help alleviate long-term price pressures. However, this clashes with the current government's stance; Energy Secretary Ed Miliband recently dismissed calls for new North Sea licences, arguing that international markets dictate prices regardless of domestic production.

As the conflict continues to disrupt the vital Strait of Hormuz, the UK government asserts it is monitoring the situation "hour-by-hour." Consumers, meanwhile, are left bracing for the forecasted £332 surge in their energy costs come July, waiting to see if targeted state interventions will be enough to soften the blow.
